title: Braid Stability for Periodic Orbits of Area-preserving Surface Diffeomorphisms
speaker: Michael Hutchings
abstract: Given an area-preserving surface diffeomorphism, what can one say about the topological properties of its periodic orbits? In particular, a finite set of periodic orbits gives rise to a braid in the mapping torus, and one can ask which isotopy classes of braids arise this way. We show that under some nondegeneracy hypotheses, the isotopy classes of braids that arise from finite sets of periodic orbits are stable under Hamiltonian perturbations that are small with respect to the Hofer metric.

proof_stages.label: Mapping-torus braid stability problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk narrows the general question of stability of knot and link types of periodic orbits to the suspension vector field on the mapping torus of a nondegenerate area-preserving surface diffeomorphism, and defines braid types as oriented links transverse to the fibers.

proof_stages.label: Hamiltonian perturbations and main theorem
proof_stages.summary: A Hamiltonian on the mapping torus defines a perturbed diffeomorphism phi_h and a canonical diffeomorphism between mapping tori, and the main theorem asserts that for each finite simple orbit set alpha there is a Hofer-smallness threshold delta forcing an isotopic simple orbit set for phi_h.

proof_stages.label: Holomorphic-current setup
proof_stages.summary: The proof chooses PFH/ECH-type almost complex structures on R times the mapping torus, defines orbit sets and relative homology classes, and introduces moduli spaces of J-holomorphic currents asymptotic to chosen orbit sets.

proof_stages.label: Action, ECH-index, and partition constraints
proof_stages.summary: Holomorphic currents satisfy a nonnegative action inequality, generic currents have nonnegative ECH index and embeddedness/Fredholm-index control, and the ECH partition conditions constrain the multiplicities of their asymptotic ends.

proof_stages.label: Hamiltonian graph cobordism and small sandwich
proof_stages.summary: Graphs of Hamiltonians in R times the original mapping torus form a symplectic cobordism between the corresponding perturbed mapping tori, allowing a Hofer-small Hamiltonian to be sandwiched between 0 and a small constant so that action bounds exclude unwanted extra levels at the ends.

proof_stages.label: Neck stretching from trivial cylinders
proof_stages.summary: Starting with the unique class-zero union of trivial cylinders from alpha to alpha in the composite cobordism, the proof stretches the neck along the intermediate hypersurface Y_{phi_h} to obtain a broken building with an intermediate orbit set beta.

proof_stages.label: Parity and simplicity conclusion
proof_stages.summary: If the intermediate orbit set beta is simple then embedded cylinders give the desired braid isotopy, while if beta is not simple the ECH partition conditions make such breakings occur in even number; since the original count is odd, at least one simple beta must occur.

examples.name: Classical closed braids in a solid torus
examples.note: The usual braid picture in a disk times an interval, closed up to a solid torus when the base surface is a disk, makes the mapping-torus definition of a braid concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker invokes classical closed braids in a solid torus as the familiar knot-theory model for the generalized braid notion, but does not compute or analyze a particular closed braid.

passes.reason: Ordinary closed braids in a solid torus are used only as the classical motivating instance of the general braid notion.

examples.name: Changing the rotation number on a torus
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker mentions perturbing a rational torus rotation to an irrational or Diophantine one as a cautionary illustration that non-Hamiltonian variants can fail.
examples.engagement_reason: Changing the rotation number on a torus is raised only as a quick illustrative failure mode for non-Hamiltonian isotopies, with no construction or calculation carried out.

passes.reason: A rigid rational torus rotation, degenerate through its nonisolated periodic behavior, is invoked to break a hoped-for non-Hamiltonian stability statement by changing the rotation number.
a rigid rational torus rotation is dynamically degenerate and is used to break a possible non-Hamiltonian stability statement by changing the rotation number
A rational torus rotation is a highly regular degenerate rotation, and changing its rotation number is presented as a way non-Hamiltonian perturbations can break the desired stability.
A rigid rational torus rotation is highly resonant with non-isolated periodic behavior and is invoked to show non-Hamiltonian stability can fail.
A rigid rational torus rotation has collapsed periodic families, and changing its rotation number is invoked as a way non-Hamiltonian stability can fail.
A resonant rational torus rotation is a degenerate highly regular case, and changing its rotation number is invoked to break a non-Hamiltonian stability extension.
Changing a rational torus rotation to an irrational one is offered as a way a non-Hamiltonian version can fail; the rational rotation is dynamically degenerate and highly regular.
Used as an obstruction to extending the stability statement beyond the Hamiltonian setting, via rigid torus rotations whose periodic dynamics is non-generic/degenerate.
Changing a torus rotation number is offered as a way to break a non-Hamiltonian stability analogue, using highly regular torus rotations.
Changing a torus rotation number is invoked as a way to break the desired stability outside the Hamiltonian setting, and the rational rigid rotation case has non-isolated periodic behavior.
Invoked as a way non-Hamiltonian perturbations can break stability by changing a rational torus rotation to an irrational one, and the rigid rational rotation is dynamically degenerate.
used as an obstruction to a non-Hamiltonian stability statement via highly symmetric torus rotations with altered rotation number

examples.name: C0-small perturbation via graph-diagonal intersection
examples.note: The speaker contrasts the theorem with the easier case where the map itself is C0-close, so nondegenerate periodic points persist by a local linking/intersection argument with the diagonal.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker gives a concrete local argument: a periodic point is an intersection of the diagonal with the graph of an iterate, nondegeneracy gives a small circle with linking number ±1, and this forces an intersection after a C0-small perturbation.

passes.reason: The graph-diagonal intersection is a standard transverse local persistence argument for C0-small perturbations, not an adversarial example.
standard transverse graph-diagonal fixed-point intersection used to explain why C0-small stability is straightforward
The graph-diagonal intersection argument is a standard local persistence mechanism for nondegenerate periodic points under C0-small perturbation.
The graph-diagonal intersection is a standard transverse nondegenerate fixed-point argument for why C0-small stability is ordinary.
The graph-diagonal intersection argument is a generic local persistence mechanism for nondegenerate periodic points, not a disproof case.

Used as a positive comparison explaining why C0-small map perturbations preserve a nondegenerate periodic point by a standard transverse graph-diagonal intersection.
A generic transverse graph-diagonal intersection argument is used to explain ordinary C0 persistence, not to refute a claim.
The graph-diagonal intersection argument is a standard local explanation of C0-stability of a nondegenerate periodic point, not an adversarial example.
Used to explain the easy C0-stability mechanism via a generic transverse graph-diagonal intersection rather than to refute a claim.
generic transverse graph-diagonal intersection used as an easy persistence argument under C0-small perturbation

examples.name: Hyperbolic ECH partition patterns
examples.note: Positive hyperbolic orbits give a partition into single ends, while negative hyperbolic orbits give double-cover patterns, illustrating the ECH partition restrictions later used to control multiply covered breakings.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker spells out the positive and negative hyperbolic ECH partition patterns and then uses the partition conditions in the proof to control multiplicities and rule/count non-simple breakings.

passes.reason: The hyperbolic ECH partitions are the extreme regular patterns such as all ones or all twos-plus-one, used constructively to force simple orbit sets.
the hyperbolic ECH partitions are regular boundary patterns such as all 1s or all 2s used as a proof ingredient
The hyperbolic ECH partitions are rigid all-1 or all-2-plus-1 extreme regular partition patterns used constructively to force simple intermediate orbit sets.
The hyperbolic ECH partitions are boundary/fine patterns such as all ones or parity-forced twos, used as proof constraints rather than as a failure case.
The hyperbolic ECH partitions are uniform all-ones or all-twos patterns, with the all-ones case the finest possible partition, used as proof input.
The hyperbolic ECH partitions such as all ones or twos-plus-possibly-one are boundary/simple partition patterns used to control breakings, not to refute a claim.
The hyperbolic ECH partitions are used as proof constraints, and structurally they are the systematic extremal regular patterns such as all ones or repeated twos.
Used constructively in the proof, and the hyperbolic ECH partitions are boundary partition patterns such as all ones or twos-plus-one rather than sporadic exceptions.

The hyperbolic ECH partition patterns are cited as structural partition conditions, with the all-ones/all-twos patterns lying at simple extremal ends among partitions.
